does: This paper proposes MLJailDe, a multilingual jailbreak detection framework that addresses the safety gap in large language models across non-English languages. The core contribution is a two-part approach: first, a back-translation data augmentation algorithm that creates semantically consistent jailbreak and benign samples across 11 languages (totaling 2,232 benign and 1,239 jailbreak examples), and second, a training method using relative-distance constraints to reduce representation dispersion across languages. This encourages jailbreak prompts with similar malicious intent to cluster together regardless of language, improving both within-language robustness and cross-lingual generalization of detection models.
connects: This work builds on the rapidly growing literature identifying that LLM safety training concentrates on dominant languages like English, leaving multilingual systems vulnerable to attacks. It extends prior jailbreak detection research—which has primarily focused on English-only defenses—by adapting multilingual representation learning techniques and cross-lingual transfer methods. The paper responds to known challenges in multilingual NLP: representation dispersion across languages and limited multilingual supervision. It combines back-translation augmentation strategies from machine translation with contrastive learning approaches for debiasing, applying these techniques to the emerging problem of multilingual adversarial robustness.
enables: This work enables more robust safety practices for globally deployed LLMs by establishing both a multilingual jailbreak detection benchmark and an effective detection framework. It points toward developing safety measures that scale across linguistic variation without requiring equivalent supervision in all languages. Future directions include expanding the dataset and language coverage, integrating these detection mechanisms into deployed multilingual systems, and extending the approach to other safety risks beyond jailbreaking. The framework provides infrastructure for studying how adversarial attacks transfer across languages and could inform broader multilingual alignment strategies as LLMs are increasingly used by non-English speakers.
